persons.name: Elinor Carlisle
persons.notes: Charged with murdering Mary Gerrard; Poirot proves her innocent

persons.name: Mary Gerrard
persons.notes: Niece of Elinor's aunt's lodgekeeper; stood to inherit from the aunt

persons.name: Nurse Hopkins
persons.notes: Killed Mary Gerrard using morphine stolen from her nursing bag

deaths.cause_detail: Morphine introduced into fish-paste sandwiches during a tea visit; Elinor Carlisle was present and was blamed, but the morphine came from Nurse Hopkins's stolen supply
deaths.motive_detail: Nurse Hopkins and Mary were related (Hopkins was her mother); Hopkins killed Mary to prevent her inheriting wealth that Hopkins believed she herself deserved
deaths.notes: Twist: the killer is Nurse Hopkins, not the accused Elinor Carlisle

persons.name: Henry Morley
persons.notes: Poirot's own dentist; killed because he had recognised someone

persons.name: Alistair Blunt
persons.notes: Orchestrated the deaths to protect his identity and his conservative financial empire

persons.name: Amberiotis
persons.notes: Died from an anaesthetic overdose in Morley's dental chair

deaths.cause_detail: Died in Henry Morley's dental chair from an overdose of local anaesthetic; initially appeared to be dental malpractice
deaths.motive_detail: Morley killed Amberiotis under instruction — or the death was arranged by others to silence a blackmailer
deaths.notes: The exact chain of responsibility is complex; Morley may have acted under duress

deaths.cause_detail: Found shot dead in his surgery; staged to appear as suicide
deaths.motive_detail: Morley had recognised Blunt from years earlier under a different identity

persons.name: Arlena Marshall
persons.notes: Flamboyant actress at a seaside resort; appeared to be having an affair with Patrick Redfern

persons.name: Patrick Redfern
persons.notes: Appeared infatuated with Arlena; actually conspiring with his wife to kill her

persons.name: Christine Redfern
persons.notes: Patrick's wife; provided his alibi by impersonating Arlena while he killed her

deaths.cause_detail: Strangled on a secluded beach cove; Patrick Redfern appeared to discover the body, but Christine had impersonated Arlena from a distance to give Patrick a false timeline
deaths.motive_detail: Redfern and Christine had conspired: Arlena had signed her assets over to Redfern and had to be killed before she could revoke this
deaths.notes: Twist: the apparent flirtation was an elaborate setup; Christine Redfern impersonated Arlena in a red bathing cap to create a false alibi

title: Five Little Pigs
creator: Agatha Christie
notes: Poirot investigates a 16-year-old case through witness memories alone

persons.name: Amyas Crale
persons.notes: Famous painter; was having an affair with his model Elsa Greer

persons.name: Caroline Crale
persons.notes: Convicted of her husband's murder; died in prison; actually innocent — protected her half-sister

persons.name: Angela Warren
persons.notes: Caroline's half-sister; accidentally introduced coniine into the beer

persons.name: Elsa Greer
persons.notes: Amyas's model and lover; one of the five suspects

deaths.cause_detail: Coniine introduced into his beer glass while he was painting; died while working at his easel
deaths.motive_detail: Angela, then a teenager, was furious at Amyas for a perceived injury; the poisoning was impulsive and she did not understand the full consequences
deaths.notes: Twist: Caroline was convicted but innocent; she took the blame to protect young Angela. The death is classified as manslaughter given Angela's age and impulsive state.

deaths.cause_detail: Died in prison of natural causes while serving a sentence for the murder of her husband
deaths.notes: Backstory death; occurs before the novel's main action

persons.name: Dr. John Christow
persons.notes: Brilliant doctor; having an affair with sculptor Henrietta Savernake

persons.name: Gerda Christow
persons.notes: John's devoted wife; shot him by the swimming pool after discovering his infidelity

persons.name: Henrietta Savernake
persons.notes: John's lover; tried to protect Gerda by throwing the gun into the pool

persons.name: Veronica Cray
persons.notes: John's former obsessive love interest; visited The Hollow and reignited tensions

deaths.cause_detail: Shot by the swimming pool at The Hollow; Gerda stood over him holding the gun when Poirot arrived; he gasped 'Henrietta' as he died
deaths.motive_detail: Gerda discovered John's affair with Henrietta and shot him in a moment of overwhelming jealousy
deaths.notes: Henrietta immediately threw the gun into the pool to protect Gerda; the apparent murderer is the actual killer

deaths.cause_detail: Took a fatal overdose of sleeping tablets after realising Poirot had identified her as the killer

persons.name: Gordon Cloade
persons.notes: Killed in a wartime bombing; his new young wife inherited everything

persons.name: Rosaleen Cloade
persons.notes: Gordon's second wife who inherited his entire fortune; possibly a bigamist

persons.name: David Hunter
persons.notes: Rosaleen's brother; manipulative and protective of the inheritance

persons.name: Rowley Cloade
persons.notes: Gordon's nephew; killed Enoch Arden to protect the Cloade family's finances

deaths.cause_detail: Killed in a wartime bombing raid in London; his unexpected death left his entire estate to his new wife Rosaleen, cutting off his extended family
deaths.notes: Backstory death; the inciting event for the novel's inheritance disputes

deaths.cause_detail: Bludgeoned at the local inn; Arden had claimed to know that Rosaleen's first husband Robert Underhay was still alive, making her marriage to Gordon bigamous and voiding the will
deaths.motive_detail: If Arden exposed Rosaleen's bigamy and Underhay were alive, the Cloade family would recover Gordon's fortune; Rowley killed Arden to silence him

deaths.cause_detail: Died from a drug overdose; circumstances suggest she was murdered by her own brother David Hunter
deaths.motive_detail: David needed to control the estate and feared Rosaleen would expose their secrets

persons.name: Mrs. McGinty
persons.notes: Killed because she recognised a photograph linking a local resident to an old murder case

persons.name: James Bentley
persons.notes: Convicted of Mrs. McGinty's murder; Poirot proves him innocent

persons.name: Evelyn Hope
persons.notes: Daughter of Eva Kane; killed Mrs. McGinty to conceal her mother's criminal past

deaths.cause_detail: Bludgeoned to death in her cottage; she had found a newspaper photograph linking a local person to the historical Craig murder case and showed it to her lodger James Bentley
deaths.motive_detail: McGinty had recognised that Evelyn Hope was the daughter of Eva Kane, a woman involved in the historical 'Craig' child murder case; exposure would have destroyed Evelyn's life

persons.name: Richard Abernethie
persons.notes: Died of gastroenteritis; actually poisoned

persons.name: Cora Lansquenet
persons.notes: Said at the funeral 'But he was murdered, wasn't he?' — killed the next day to silence her

persons.name: Miss Gilchrist
persons.notes: Cora's companion; killed both Cora and Richard to inherit; impersonated Cora after killing her

deaths.cause_detail: Poisoned with arsenic by Miss Gilchrist; death appeared to be from gastroenteritis
deaths.motive_detail: Gilchrist had been mentioned in Richard's will; she killed him to inherit and silenced anyone who might suspect
deaths.notes: Twist: Richard's death was initially believed natural; Cora's blurted remark at the funeral sets off the investigation

deaths.cause_detail: Hacked to death with a hatchet in her cottage the day after Richard's funeral; she had blurted out at the funeral that Richard had been murdered
deaths.motive_detail: Cora had unwittingly revealed she knew Richard was murdered; she had to be silenced

persons.name: Celia Austin
persons.notes: Had been stealing small items — compulsive behaviour linked to anxiety; murdered when she knew too much

persons.name: Nigel Chapman
persons.notes: Running a drug-smuggling operation out of the student hostel

persons.name: Patricia Lane
persons.notes: Killed after she became a threat to the smuggling operation

deaths.cause_detail: Killed with a morphine overdose staged as suicide; she had discovered the drug-smuggling operation and was becoming a threat
deaths.motive_detail: Celia had discovered Chapman's drug-smuggling activities hidden in student luggage

deaths.cause_detail: Struck on the head and killed; she had become suspicious of Nigel Chapman
deaths.motive_detail: Patricia was investigating the smuggling and getting too close to the truth

persons.name: Marlene Tucker
persons.notes: Local girl cast as the 'victim' in a charity murder-hunt game; actually murdered

persons.name: Hattie Stubbs
persons.notes: Wife of Sir George Stubbs; disappeared during the fete; actually killed by her husband

persons.name: Sir George Stubbs
persons.notes: Killed his wife Hattie and Marlene Tucker to stage events at the fete

persons.name: Ariadne Oliver
persons.notes: Called Poirot because something about the murder-hunt 'felt wrong'

deaths.cause_detail: Killed by her husband Sir George Stubbs before the fete and her body concealed
deaths.motive_detail: George had married Hattie for her money; she had to die before she could change her will
deaths.notes: Twist: the 'missing' Hattie had already been killed; her disappearance was staged to appear as a voluntary flight

deaths.cause_detail: Strangled in the boathouse while acting as the 'murder victim' in the fete's murder-hunt game
deaths.motive_detail: Marlene had seen something that could expose George's role in Hattie's disappearance

persons.name: Hercule Poirot
persons.notes: Appears only in the final third of the novel

persons.name: Miss Springer
persons.notes: Found the hidden jewels of Ramat in the sports equipment store

persons.name: Ann Shapland
persons.notes: Working for a criminal organisation seeking the Ramat jewels

persons.name: Miss Bulstrode
persons.notes: Head of Meadowbank school; suspects something is wrong

deaths.cause_detail: Shot in the sports equipment room at Meadowbank school; had discovered the valuable Ramat jewels hidden in a tennis racquet handle
deaths.motive_detail: Shapland was an agent sent to retrieve the Ramat royal jewels smuggled into the school; Springer had to be silenced

title: The Clocks
creator: Agatha Christie
notes: Poirot investigates from his armchair; unusual structure with a spy-thriller subplot

persons.name: Hercule Poirot
persons.notes: Primarily investigates from his flat, consulting the case files

persons.name: Colin Lamb
persons.notes: Son of Superintendent Battle; reports to Poirot

persons.name: Sheila Webb
persons.notes: Found the body of an unknown man in a blind woman's sitting room

persons.name: Ramsey
persons.notes: An unknown man found dead; connected to a Cold War espionage network

persons.name: Josefina Bland
persons.notes: Part of the spy network; killed to silence witnesses

deaths.cause_detail: Stabbed and found in the sitting room of blind Miss Pebmarsh surrounded by stopped clocks; the clocks were placed as a deliberate red herring
deaths.motive_detail: Ramsey had discovered the spy network operating in Crowdean and had to be eliminated
deaths.notes: The stopped clocks and blind woman's home are carefully staged red herrings

persons.name: Norma Restarick
persons.notes: Believes she may have committed murder; actually being drugged and manipulated by her stepmother

persons.name: Mary Restarick
persons.notes: Conspiring with her lover to gain Andrew Restarick's fortune; poisoning Norma to drive her to suicide or have her committed

deaths.cause_detail: Poisoned by his wife Mary and her accomplice
deaths.motive_detail: Mary and her lover planned to inherit Andrew's fortune

persons.name: Joyce Reynolds
persons.notes: Boasted at the party that she had once witnessed a murder; drowned minutes later

persons.name: Ariadne Oliver
persons.notes: Called Poirot after Joyce's murder

persons.name: Rowena Drake
persons.notes: Had killed her au pair years earlier; killed Joyce and Leopold to silence them

deaths.cause_detail: Head held down in the apple-bobbing tub at a Halloween party after she boasted of having witnessed a murder years before
deaths.motive_detail: Joyce had witnessed Rowena Drake murder her au pair girl years earlier; Drake killed her to prevent exposure

deaths.cause_detail: Drowned in a stream; Joyce's brother had also witnessed or knew something connected to the original murder
deaths.motive_detail: Leopold knew something that could have confirmed Joyce's original claim

persons.name: Ariadne Oliver
persons.notes: Drives the investigation by gathering witness memories

persons.name: General Ravenscroft
persons.notes: Died in an apparent double shooting with his wife on a clifftop

persons.name: Lady Ravenscroft
persons.notes: The truth: the woman who died was Lady Ravenscroft's mentally ill twin sister, not Lady Ravenscroft herself

deaths.cause_detail: Found shot dead on a clifftop alongside his wife in what appeared to be a mutual shooting or suicide pact; investigated retrospectively years later
deaths.motive_detail: Lady Ravenscroft killed her husband and then herself to end an impossible situation involving her mentally ill twin sister who had been impersonating her

deaths.cause_detail: Shot herself after killing her husband
deaths.notes: Twist: the woman who died in the clifftop incident was actually Lady Ravenscroft's twin sister (who had been living as Lady Ravenscroft due to the real Lady Ravenscroft's mental illness); the identities of the two women on the cliff are a central mystery

title: Curtain: Poirot's Last Case
creator: Agatha Christie
notes: Written in the 1940s but withheld for publication after Christie's death; Poirot's final case

persons.name: Hercule Poirot
persons.notes: Dies at the end of the novel; shoots Norton himself and then allows his heart to fail by stopping his medication

persons.name: Arthur Hastings
persons.notes: Narrator; does not realise until the posthumous letter that Poirot killed Norton

persons.name: Stephen Norton
persons.notes: Identified by Poirot as 'X': an instigator who psychologically manipulates susceptible people into committing murders

persons.name: Barbara Franklin
persons.notes: Poisoned by her own husband after Norton manipulated him

deaths.cause_detail: Poisoned with physostigmine in her coffee by her husband Dr. Franklin, who was manipulated into murder by Stephen Norton
deaths.motive_detail: Norton psychologically manipulated Franklin into believing his wife was an obstacle; Franklin acted at Norton's suggestion

deaths.cause_detail: Shot through the middle of his forehead while in bed; Poirot fired through the interconnecting wall between their rooms at Styles Court
deaths.motive_detail: Poirot determined that Norton was legally untouchable — he never committed crimes himself, only instigated them — and executed him to prevent future murders
deaths.notes: Twist: the killer is Poirot himself; revealed posthumously in a letter to Hastings

deaths.cause_detail: Died of heart failure after deliberately discontinuing his heart medication following Norton's execution; chose to die rather than live as an invalid who had committed murder
deaths.notes: Poirot's death — one of detective fiction's most famous endings

persons.name: Colonel Protheroe
persons.notes: Disliked by almost everyone in St. Mary Mead; his death was welcomed by many

persons.name: Anne Protheroe
persons.notes: Killed her husband to be with her lover Lawrence Redding

persons.name: Lawrence Redding
persons.notes: Anne's lover; confessed falsely to protect Anne

persons.name: Reverend Leonard Clement
persons.notes: Narrator of the novel; the vicar in whose study Protheroe was shot

deaths.cause_detail: Shot in the head in the vicar's study; both his wife Anne and her lover Lawrence Redding confessed, but the actual killer is Anne
deaths.motive_detail: Anne wanted to be free of her husband to be with Lawrence Redding
deaths.notes: Two false confessions complicated the investigation; Miss Marple deduced the truth from the forged time on a letter found at the scene

persons.name: Ruby Keene
persons.notes: Found strangled in Colonel Bantry's library; was being cultivated by wealthy Conway Jefferson

persons.name: Pamela Reeves
persons.notes: Killed and burned in a car to provide a false identity switch with Ruby Keene

persons.name: Mark Gaskell
persons.notes: Son-in-law of Conway Jefferson; conspired with Josie Turner to kill Ruby and Pamela

persons.name: Josie Turner
persons.notes: Ruby Keene's cousin; feared being supplanted; conspired with Mark Gaskell

deaths.cause_detail: Strangled and placed in Colonel Bantry's library to misdirect suspicion; Conway Jefferson intended to change his will in her favour
deaths.motive_detail: Conway Jefferson planned to adopt Ruby and leave her his fortune, cutting out his existing heirs Mark Gaskell and Adelaide Jefferson

deaths.cause_detail: Killed and her body burned beyond recognition in a car to serve as a false 'Ruby Keene' — allowing the killers to establish that Ruby had eloped and died in an accident
deaths.motive_detail: Pamela was killed to create a false second body that could be misidentified as Ruby's in a burned car
deaths.notes: Twist: the burned body in the car is not Ruby Keene but an innocent girl guide; Miss Marple identifies the switch through nail varnish and other details

persons.name: Miss Marple
persons.notes: Appears only late in the novel

persons.name: Mrs. Symmington
persons.notes: Received a poison-pen letter and apparently committed suicide; actually murdered

persons.name: Megan Hunter
persons.notes: Mrs. Symmington's stepdaughter; narrator's love interest

persons.name: Mr. Symmington
persons.notes: Wrote the poison-pen letters to cover the real murder of his wife; wanted to marry his children's governess Elsie Holland

persons.name: Agnes Woddell
persons.notes: Killed because she recognised the letter-writer

deaths.cause_detail: Took what appeared to be a suicide dose of cyanide after receiving an anonymous letter; actually murdered — her husband had sent the letter knowing she would take it as proof of an affair and die rather than face the shame
deaths.motive_detail: Symmington wanted to marry his children's governess Elsie Holland; he wrote the poison-pen letters to cover this specific murder
deaths.notes: Twist: the poison-pen letters were written by the solicitor himself — not an anonymous malicious villager — specifically to murder his wife under cover of a general harassment campaign

deaths.cause_detail: Strangled in the Symmington house; she had recognised the handwriting on the letters and confronted the killer
deaths.motive_detail: Agnes knew who had written the poison-pen letters

persons.name: Letitia Blacklock
persons.notes: Actually Charlotte Blacklock, impersonating her dead sister Letitia to inherit Randall Goedler's fortune

persons.name: Rudi Scherz
persons.notes: Hired to burst in with a gun and create a diversion; shot dead by the real killer

persons.name: Dora Bunner
persons.notes: Charlotte's old schoolfriend; accidentally began to reveal Charlotte's true identity

deaths.cause_detail: Shot dead in the dark during a staged 'announcement' at Little Paddocks; Scherz was meant to fire blanks and flee, but was shot by the real killer using a second gun
deaths.motive_detail: Scherz knew too much about the killer's identity and plans; the staged event gave an opportunity to kill him and the actual target (Miss Blacklock) at once

deaths.cause_detail: Her aspirin tablets replaced with a fatal drug; she was on the verge of unwittingly revealing Charlotte's true identity
deaths.motive_detail: Bunner's memories of the real Letitia Blacklock were beginning to emerge in her conversation; she had to be silenced

persons.name: Carrie Louise Serrocold
persons.notes: Miss Marple's old friend; her charitable rehabilitation institution is the setting

persons.name: Christian Gulbrandsen
persons.notes: Discovered that money was being embezzled from the institution

persons.name: Lewis Serrocold
persons.notes: Carrie Louise's husband; the embezzler and killer

deaths.cause_detail: Shot during the carefully staged distraction of a young delinquent's attack on Lewis Serrocold; the disturbance was manufactured to give Serrocold an alibi
deaths.motive_detail: Gulbrandsen had discovered that Serrocold was embezzling the charitable trust's funds and had to be stopped before he could expose him
deaths.notes: Twist: the entire violent incident with the young offender was staged as cover for the murder

persons.name: Miss Marple
persons.notes: Comes to investigate because of Gladys Martin, a former maid she trained

persons.name: Rex Fortescue
persons.notes: Died at his city office; rye grain found in his pocket (the blackbirds baked in a pie)

persons.name: Adele Fortescue
persons.notes: Rex's young second wife; poisoned while having tea (the queen eating bread and honey)

persons.name: Gladys Martin
persons.notes: Strangled and found with a clothes peg on her nose (the maid hanging out the clothes)

persons.name: Percival Fortescue
persons.notes: Rex's elder son; poisoned his father with taxine from yew trees to inherit

deaths.cause_detail: Taxine introduced into his marmalade at breakfast; died at his city office; rye was found sewn into his coat pocket
deaths.motive_detail: Percival wanted to inherit the Fortescue fortune and take full control of the family business
deaths.notes: The nursery rhyme 'Sing a Song of Sixpence' structure runs through all three murders

deaths.cause_detail: Cyanide introduced into her afternoon tea; found dead in the drawing room
deaths.motive_detail: Adele as widow would have taken a large portion of the estate; Percival eliminated her

deaths.cause_detail: Strangled in the garden with a clothes peg placed on her nose; she had witnessed something that could identify the killer
deaths.motive_detail: Gladys had seen or knew something incriminating about Percival

title: 4:50 from Paddington
creator: Agatha Christie
notes: Also published as 'What Mrs. McGillicuddy Saw!'

persons.name: Miss Marple
persons.notes: Sends her friend Lucy Eyelesbarrow to investigate undercover

persons.name: Mrs. McGillicuddy
persons.notes: Saw a woman being strangled in a passing train; the only witness

persons.name: Martine Crackenthorpe
persons.notes: Her body is eventually found in a sarcophagus at Rutherford Hall

persons.name: Edmund Crackenthorpe
persons.notes: Son of Luther Crackenthorpe; poisoned

persons.name: Alfred Crackenthorpe
persons.notes: Killed Martine and Edmund to protect the estate from being divided

deaths.cause_detail: Strangled on a train and her body dumped at Rutherford Hall; Mrs. McGillicuddy witnessed the murder through the train window
deaths.motive_detail: Martine was the widow of the eldest Crackenthorpe son Edmund; she and her son would have inherited a large share of the Crackenthorpe estate

deaths.cause_detail: Poisoned at the family meal
deaths.motive_detail: Edmund was suspicious of Alfred; Alfred poisoned him to remove a rival heir and potential witness

title: The Mirror Crack'd from Side to Side
creator: Agatha Christie

persons.name: Marina Gregg
persons.notes: Killed Heather Badcock — the woman whose innocent action caused Marina's baby to be born with congenital rubella

persons.name: Heather Badcock
persons.notes: Eagerly told Marina how she had gone to meet her years ago despite having German measles, which infected the pregnant Marina and caused her baby's disability

persons.name: Jason Rudd
persons.notes: Marina's husband; suspected the truth but covered for his wife

deaths.cause_detail: Marina Gregg had poisoned her own cocktail intending to kill herself; Heather Badcock picked up Marina's glass by mistake and drank the poisoned cocktail
deaths.motive_detail: Marina recognised Heather as the woman who, by visiting her while ill with German measles, caused Marina's unborn child to be born severely disabled
deaths.notes: Twist: the intended victim was not Heather Badcock but Marina herself — Heather died by accidentally taking Marina's poisoned drink

deaths.cause_detail: Died of a drug overdose; by the end of the novel she has been identified as the killer

persons.name: Major Palgrave
persons.notes: Killed before he could show Miss Marple a photograph of a known murderer

persons.name: Molly Kendal
persons.notes: Runs the Golden Palm hotel with her husband Tim

persons.name: Jason Rafiel
persons.notes: Disabled millionaire who helps Miss Marple

persons.name: Lucky Dyson
persons.notes: Has murdered two previous husbands; conspiring with Edward Hillingdon

deaths.cause_detail: His usual heart medication was replaced with an overdose; died in his sleep; he had been about to show Miss Marple a photograph of a murderer
deaths.motive_detail: Palgrave was about to identify Lucky's photograph as that of a known wife-murderer

deaths.cause_detail: A local worker who had seen something relevant to Major Palgrave's death; killed to prevent her speaking
deaths.motive_detail: Victoria had witnessed something that could link the killers to Major Palgrave's death

persons.name: Michael Gorman
persons.notes: Actually Bess Sedgwick's former husband; knew too much about the criminal operation

persons.name: Bess Sedgwick
persons.notes: Famous for daredevil exploits; the brains behind the gang using Bertram's Hotel as a criminal base

persons.name: Elvira Blake
persons.notes: Bess Sedgwick's secret daughter; targeted for murder by an accomplice

deaths.cause_detail: Shot in the street outside Bertram's Hotel; was Bess Sedgwick's long-estranged husband who knew the truth about the criminal organisation based at the hotel
deaths.motive_detail: Gorman threatened to expose the criminal gang using Bertram's Hotel for robbery logistics

persons.name: Miss Marple
persons.notes: Commissioned posthumously by Jason Rafiel to investigate an unspecified crime

persons.name: Jason Rafiel
persons.notes: Died before the novel begins; left Miss Marple a legacy in return for solving 'a crime'

persons.name: Nora Broad
persons.notes: One of several girls murdered by the serial killer

persons.name: Clothilde Bradbury-Scott
persons.notes: Killed multiple young women out of obsessive jealousy; had hidden one victim's body in her garden for years

deaths.cause_detail: Killed by Clothilde Bradbury-Scott out of obsessive possessive jealousy; body hidden in the garden of The Old Manor House for years
deaths.motive_detail: Clothilde was obsessively attached to Verity and killed her when Verity fell in love with Michael Rafiel
deaths.notes: Twist: an innocent man (Michael Rafiel, Jason's son) was convicted of a different murder; the real crime was Verity Hunt's buried in the garden

deaths.cause_detail: Strangled and her body substituted for Verity Hunt's in order to frame Michael Rafiel for Verity's murder
deaths.motive_detail: Nora's body was used to fake Verity's murder in a public place, sending investigators in the wrong direction

title: Sleeping Murder
creator: Agatha Christie
notes: Written in the 1940s; Miss Marple's final case, published posthumously

persons.name: Gwenda Reed
persons.notes: As a small child witnessed her stepmother's murder; bought the same house 18 years later, triggering repressed memories

persons.name: Helen Halliday
persons.notes: Gwenda's stepmother; strangled 18 years before the main action; Gwenda witnessed it as a child

persons.name: Dr. James Kennedy
persons.notes: Helen's brother; obsessively controlling; killed Helen when she tried to leave her husband and build her own life

deaths.cause_detail: Strangled 18 years before the main action by her brother Dr. Kennedy; small Gwenda witnessed the murder but repressed the memory
deaths.motive_detail: Kennedy had an obsessive, possessive relationship with his sister Helen; he killed her rather than allow her to leave her husband and live independently
deaths.notes: Backstory murder; the entire novel builds towards uncovering this long-buried crime
